system_description.overview: Timeglass is an AI-powered time tracking and project management platform for professional services teams. It automates timesheets, provides productivity insights, and manages client billing.

products.Work Tracking & Timelines: Automated time capture and project timeline management
products.Performance & Productivity Insights: AI-driven analytics on team and individual productivity
products.Client & Project Management: Tools for managing client relationships and project delivery
products.Billing & Invoicing: Automated billing based on tracked time and project milestones

network_architecture.network_segmentation: VPC with network segmentation; production environment isolated from development and test environments
network_architecture.firewalls: Firewalls configured to restrict unauthorized access; firewall rules managed as code
network_architecture.ids_ips: IDS/IPS provided via GCP infrastructure controls
network_architecture.vpn: Bastion host used for secure administrative access
network_architecture.tls: TLS in transit for all communications
network_architecture.topology: Internet traffic routed through GCP VPC; application services access Supabase (Postgres) database; administrative access via bastion host; GCP manages underlying physical infrastructure (carve-out)

application_architecture.pattern: Not explicitly disclosed; cloud-native on GCP
application_architecture.environments: Separate development, test, and production environments

authentication_access_control.mfa: MFA enforced for source code access and administrative accounts; Google Workspace SSO with MFA
authentication_access_control.password_policy: Managed via Google Workspace; complexity and expiry enforced

encryption.at_rest: AES-256 via GCP Cloud Storage encryption

ci_cd_devops.branch_protection: Merge approval required before merging to main branch; branch protection rules enforced
ci_cd_devops.iac: Firewall configurations managed as code; full IaC not confirmed
ci_cd_devops.patch_management: Automatic patch management; CTO responsible for overseeing patch process

third_party_services.purpose: AI/ML capabilities for time tracking automation and productivity insights

security_tools.antivirus: Antivirus deployed on endpoints; weekly definition updates

excluded_criteria: CC6.4 - Physical access controls delegated to GCP (carve-out)
A1.2 - No physical office; physical environmental controls not applicable
notable_controls: Branch protection with required merge approvals enforced in source control
Server configurations managed as code (infrastructure as code for firewall rules)
Annual BC/DR testing covering multiple loss scenarios
Formal change management process with emergency change procedure

infrastructure.rationale: Multi-AZ on GCP with VPC and managed services. No multi-region, no CDN mentioned, no explicit IaC beyond firewall configs. Supabase as managed DB adds vendor dependency.

application_architecture.rationale: Limited disclosure. Separate environments and branch protection confirmed but no language, framework, or architecture pattern disclosed. GCP-native compute implied.

data_layer.rationale: Supabase (Postgres) + GCS for file storage. Data classification and retention policies in place. Multi-AZ. No caching layer or data warehouse mentioned. Backup frequency not stated.

security_posture.rationale: Monthly vuln scans + annual pen test + antivirus + IDS/IPS + server hardening + MFA. No WAF explicitly mentioned. No SIEM named. Good baseline security controls.

devops_maturity.rationale: Branch protection, separate environments, formal change management, and patch management confirmed. CI/CD tool not named, IaC only partial (firewall configs). Emergency change process in place.

bcdr_readiness.rationale: BCDR policy with annual review and testing, multi-AZ failover. RTO/RPO not disclosed. No multi-region failover. Daily backups implied but frequency not explicitly stated.

vendor_diversity.rationale: Heavy reliance on GCP ecosystem (GCP + Supabase hosted on GCP + Google Gemini). Three vendors but all Google-adjacent. Single cloud provider risk.

overall.rationale: Solid foundational security posture for a Type 1 report. Multi-AZ, quarterly access reviews, annual pen test, and branch protection are positives. Significant gaps in disclosed technical detail (no CI/CD tool, no languages, no explicit IaC), heavy GCP ecosystem dependency, and no WAF or SIEM named.

red_flags: No WAF explicitly mentioned despite being a cloud-hosted SaaS
All infrastructure (GCP, Supabase, Gemini) concentrated in Google ecosystem — single cloud provider risk
yellow_flags: Limited application architecture disclosure — no languages, frameworks, or architecture pattern named
IaC only partially confirmed (firewall configs); full infrastructure-as-code not stated
SOC 2 Type 1 only — no operational effectiveness demonstrated over time
green_flags: Branch protection with required merge approvals
Annual BC/DR testing covering multiple loss scenarios
MFA enforced across source control and admin accounts
key_observations: Supabase used as managed database layer — Supabase itself is a startup, adding a layer of vendor risk beyond a hyperscaler DB service
Google Gemini API is core to the AI-powered automation features — any Gemini API changes or outages directly affect product functionality
GCP carve-out approach means GCP's own SOC 2 report covers physical and environmental controls — appropriate for this deployment model
Timeglass positions itself as AI-native time tracking but report reveals relatively modest disclosed tech stack for a Series A/seed-stage product
Privacy and Processing Integrity trust criteria excluded from scope — notable for an AI-powered productivity tool that processes employee activity data
